Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This compass error is introduced by ferrous metals and electrical systems in the aircraft itself.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Deviation is aircraft-induced and is removed via the compass swing.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This is the geographic angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a given location.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Variation (declination) is charted via isogonic lines.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: These small magnets are adjusted during compass swing to null out heading errors.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Compensator screws hold small magnets used to correct for deviation.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This gyroscopic property lets a spinning rotor hold its orientation against applied forces.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Rigidity is the property that resists rotor axis change.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: These two effects combine to make a vacuum-driven DG drift off heading over time.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Earth rotation (apparent) and friction (real) are the classic drift sources.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This instrument is used as the reference to periodically correct the DG for drift.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: The magnetic compass provides the absolute heading reference used to reset the DG.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: These words are the expansion of the acronym AHRS in avionics.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: AHRS stands for Attitude Heading Reference System.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: MEMS gyros sense angular rate through this physical principle instead of using a spinning mass.
choices: Magnetic sensors detecting Earth field rotation
Laser beams shifting phase in an optical cavity
Vibrating elements deflected by the Coriolis force
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: MEMS rate gyros use Coriolis deflection of a vibrating mass.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This is the AHRS sensor type that measures straight-line acceleration along each axis.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Accelerometers measure linear motion on each sensor axis.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This is what the AHRS magnetometer provides to support heading computation.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: The magnetometer measures the direction of Earth's magnetic field.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This installation issue commonly causes magnetometer errors and erratic AHRS heading.
choices: DC current-carrying wiring near the sensor
Low oxygen level in the flight deck
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Current-carrying DC wires generate stray magnetic fields that disturb the magnetometer.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This type of AHRS sensor measures angular rate to support attitude computation.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Rate gyros output angular rate; the distractors measure other parameters.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This error characteristic causes a stationary MEMS gyro to accumulate a slow random offset.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Bias instability is the characteristic MEMS drift at zero input.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This fusion strategy keeps a modern AHRS heading output calibrated in flight.
choices: Fusing magnetometer data with GPS track
Referencing the onboard oxygen sensor output
Reading the turn coordinator ball position
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Magnetometer-plus-GPS track fusion is the standard method.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This is the likely cause when displayed attitude slowly drifts from actual aircraft attitude on a long flight.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Without alignment updates, AHRS bias drift accumulates over long flights and shows as slow attitude divergence.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: This AHRS design feature supports rapid recovery of valid attitude after a power interruption.
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: Non-volatile memory preserves alignment across power loss.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: These components should be inspected first when a slaved HSI disagrees with the magnetic compass by a steady 5 degrees.
choices: The flux detector and the compass compensator
The pitot tube and static port condition
The GPS antenna and cable connections
The audio panel and headset wiring
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: The flux detector provides the slaving source and the compensator fine-tunes the compass.

Attitude & Heading Reference.clue: These two traditional mechanical instruments are replaced when a certified AHRS is installed.
choices: The vertical speed and the sensitive altimeter
The vertical gyro and the directional gyro
The sensitive altimeter and the compass
The airspeed indicator and the tachometer
Attitude & Heading Reference.explanation: AHRS supplies attitude and heading, replacing the VG and DG.
